channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Can the U.S. End Nigeria’s Insurgency?
item.description: Despite recent successful strikes, experts doubt the campaign’s long-term success.

item.title: Kevin Warsh’s troublesome inflation in-tray
item.description: The new Fed chair will struggle to appease his colleagues, the market and Donald Trump all at once

item.title: Trump Accuses Iran of Stalling Peace Talks
item.description: At the same time, the White House is optimistic that a deal to end the war is in reach.

item.title: Trump Is Making NATO Stronger, Lithuania’s Foreign Minister Says
item.description: He also said NATO allies should “take their part” in the Iran war.

item.title: Ferrari’s electric car: divisiveness is the point
item.description: The Italian supercar brand is attempting a high-stakes manoeuvre

item.title: Rubio’s Visit Won’t Assuage India
item.description: Repairing Trump’s damage to bilateral ties will require more than a charm offensive.

item.title: Could Donald Trump save Cuba’s economy?
item.description: Even if the island were not burdened by embargoes and communism it would be a poor investment prospect

item.title: China Needs North Korea on Side
item.description: A Xi visit to Pyongyang is likely soon, after a seven-year gap.

item.title: Too much time with colleagues can sour social interaction
item.description: A study of an Antarctic crew finds that eventually time together breeds conflict

item.title: Mosquitoes can learn to associate bug spray with food
item.description: Your insect repellent may not work as well as you think

item.title: Tomorrow’s medical sensors might come served with dinner
item.description: Listening your gut could involve riboflavin batteries and toothpaste transistors

item.title: Attacking Cuba would be a huge mistake
item.description: But Donald Trump could make a deal with the communist regime

item.title: Itamar Ben-Gvir has presided over horrific abuse in Israel’s prisons
item.description: And he is proud of it

item.title: China’s Long March to Technological Supremacy
item.description: China’s R&D prowess was visible in research output, patents, PhDs, and critical technologies for years, long before crystallizing into commercial products that Western investors, commentators, and policymakers noticed. And now it’s happening again.

item.title: Would American military action against Cuba work?
item.description: All the options are fraught

item.title: Inside the Struggle to Build an Iranian Opposition
item.description: A new group is trying to create what its predecessors could not: an effective democratic force for Iran.

item.title: Japan, South Korea and Taiwan are suffering industrial rot
item.description: Artificial intelligence is concealing a China shock

item.title: North Korea Returns to the South Via Women’s Football
item.description: Under Pyongyang’s new “two states” policy, sports diplomacy has been repackaged as inter-state, not inter-Korean, exchanges.

item.title: The world’s top condom-maker is getting squeezed
item.description: Will Karex break under pressure?

item.title: The South Korea-US Alliance: What Comes After OPCON?
item.description: OPCON transfer is not an agreement on how to separate. It's a renewed commitment that makes necessary updates to a 70-year-old framework.

item.title: The West Indulges China in Its Backing for Russia Against Ukraine
item.description: With democracies having little appetite to take on any economic risk, China’s support for Russia continues, and so too does the war.

item.title: What the Trump-Xi Summit Means for Africa
item.description: The détente may well have bought the world some time that can be usefully spent strategically. How can African countries respond?

item.title: Tajikistan’s Taxman Comes for the E-Wallet
item.description: A new levy on digital transfers suspected of hiding unregistered business extends the cash-strapped government’s long habit of taxing whatever it can reach.

item.title: The Pope’s Defense of Humanity
item.description: Pope Leo XIV is right to celebrate the grandeur of humanity, to criticize and reject posthumanism, and to emphasize the essential, categorical, and insurmountable difference between humans and machines. His first encyclical is a necessary exhortation to believers and non-believers alike.

item.title: Latin America’s Agricultural Boom Is Faltering
item.description: The productivity growth that transformed agriculture across Latin America and the Caribbean has slowed sharply in recent years, with farmers increasingly relying on costly inputs rather than efficiency gains. Without sustained public investment, the region risks losing competitiveness and reversing decades of hard-won progress.

item.title: India’s External Shock Is an Economic Opportunity
item.description: Despite strong fundamentals, India’s economy has major vulnerabilities stemming from its external account. With energy flows contested, shipping routes under threat, and capital markets increasingly shaped by geopolitics, the country must leverage its relative strength to weather current shocks and build long-term resilience.

item.title: On AI, It’s the Pope vs. Trump
item.description: The Vatican has made a frontal challenge to the Trump administration’s approach to technology.

item.title: The Economic Path to Climate Justice
item.description: Policymakers have used the concept of a “just transition” to make a morally and emotionally compelling case for decarbonization. But the experiences of Ethiopia, Pakistan, and South Africa show that rapid renewable-energy uptake in emerging and developing countries often comes down to affordability.

item.title: Indonesia’s Dive Into Economic Nationalism
item.description: A messy rollout looks set to disrupt global commodity markets.

item.title: The Right Way for Europe to Spend More on Defense
item.description: America should cofinance the continent’s rearmament.

item.title: Iran and the Forever War Trap
item.description: In trying to avoid a quagmire in Iran, America found a dead end.

item.title: Nepal, India, and the Paradox of Hydro-hegemony
item.description: Nepal’s hydropower vision depends on India, both as a primary buyer and an exclusive transit route. That gives India immense power over Nepal’s ambitions.
